description:
You are acting as a container engineer who needs to tidy-up rotated log files generated by several
    micro-services.  
    The system already contains the following directory tree (all paths are absolute).

        /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceA/
        /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceB/

    Inside those folders the following files exist before you start:

        /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceA/access-2023-01-10.log
        /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceA/error-2023-01-09.log
        /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceA/current.log
        /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceB/app-2023-01-08.log
        /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceB/debug-2023-12-31.log

    • The three “2023-01-xx” files have modification times that are MORE than 7 days old.  
    • The files “current.log” and “debug-2023-12-31.log” have modification times that are LESS
      than or equal to 7 days old.

    Your goal
    ----------
    1. Using a single terminal command that combines “find” and “xargs” (you may pipe or chain
       other *single-line* utilities such as “tee” if required), locate every “*.log”
       file under “/home/user/microservices/logs” that is **older than 7 days** and delete it.  
    2. While deleting, write a textual report of *exactly* which files were removed to the file

           /home/user/deleted_logs_report.txt

       The report **must** follow ALL of these rules so that automated grading can verify it:

       a. One absolute path per line (no extra characters).  
       b. Lines must be sorted in strict lexicographic (ASCII) order.  
       c. The file must end with a single newline character (i.e., the last path is followed by “\n”).  

    No other files must be listed in the report, and the remaining newer log files must stay on disk.

    Success criteria (checked automatically)
    ----------------------------------------
    • “/home/user/deleted_logs_report.txt” exists.  
    • Its contents match the format described above and list exactly the three old log files.  
    • The three old “*.log” files are no longer present in the filesystem.  
    • The two newer log files still exist untouched.

    Remember: accomplish everything with one command that leverages “find” + “xargs”.
truth:
Initial file set (before agent starts)
    --------------------------------------
    /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceA/access-2023-01-10.log   (mtime = 60 days ago)
    /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceA/error-2023-01-09.log    (mtime = 59 days ago)
    /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceA/current.log             (mtime = 1 day ago)
    /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceB/app-2023-01-08.log      (mtime = 58 days ago)
    /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceB/debug-2023-12-31.log    (mtime = 0 days ago)

    Expected final state after correct solution
    -------------------------------------------
    1. Files that must be completely absent:
       /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceA/access-2023-01-10.log
       /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceA/error-2023-01-09.log
       /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceB/app-2023-01-08.log

    2. Files that must still exist:
       /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceA/current.log
       /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceB/debug-2023-12-31.log

    3. Verification file that must exist with **exact** content
       Path: /home/user/deleted_logs_report.txt

       Content (with a single trailing newline, no extra blank line):

       /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceA/access-2023-01-10.log
       /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceA/error-2023-01-09.log
       /home/user/microservices/logs/serviceB/app-2023-01-08.log

       (total 3 lines, sorted, followed by newline)

    4. Permissions: the agent runs as user “user”; all directories above are owned by “user”
       and writable (0755 for directories, 0644 for files) so no root access is required.
